questions.scenario: The ticketing subscription usually lands near 8,000 USD per month, but the month-to-date forecast now shows 13,500 USD. Three product teams share the subscription, and finance wants owner-level evidence before anyone changes production resources. What is the most defensible first move?

options.text: Open Cost Analysis at the relevant scope, compare the current period with the prior one, and group spend by service, resource, and date until the increase has a clear owner.
Group by service only, pick the largest increase, and approve the first cost recommendation before drilling into the specific resources and dates involved.
Filter to one suspected product team first and compare only that slice, even though the current increase has not yet been tied to one owner.
Review the budget and forecast settings first to see whether alert thresholds changed, then inspect billed resources only if the settings still look normal.

questions.explanation: Cost work starts by making the increase visible at the right scope and drilling from a large total into service, resource, and time. The closest distractor is the immediate Advisor action, but the article is explicit that recommendations still need workload context before approval. Region moves and tighter budgets may matter later, yet neither identifies what already changed this month. Official docs: [Common uses for Cost Analysis](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/cost-management-billing/costs/cost-analysis-common-uses).

questions.scenario: A storage account spike begins on the same day a lifecycle rule was disabled. The review already knows the subscription and team. Which Cost Analysis view is most useful for proving whether this change lines up with the extra spend?

options.text: A month-to-date cost view for the whole subscription grouped only by service so storage remains visible but individual resources and meters are collapsed.
A tag-focused view for the known team that omits meter detail because ownership has already been established.
Daily cost by the affected resource and meter around the change window.
A cost view grouped by resource group for the full month without narrowing to the affected storage account or the days around the change.

questions.explanation: The useful view is the one that lines resource, meter, and date up with the suspected change. The closest distractor is the tag-only view, because tags can help with ownership, but they do not replace the resource and meter detail needed to prove this lifecycle-rule story. A subscription total and unrelated CPU telemetry do not isolate the storage cost driver. Official docs: [Common uses for Cost Analysis](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/cost-management-billing/costs/cost-analysis-common-uses).

questions.scenario: A monthly review shows production and sandbox charges blended together. Both environments live in one subscription, several resources are untagged, and the dashboard cannot reliably tell finance which team owns the expensive workspace. What design gap should the review call out?

options.text: The naming convention alone, because finance can separate production and sandbox costs from resource names even if tags and scopes are inconsistent.
The subscription, resource-group, and tagging strategy needed to separate accountability by workload and environment.
The dashboard layout, because clearer charts can compensate for mixed environments and missing ownership metadata.
The budget thresholds for each cost center, because alerts can stand in for workload and environment boundaries during monthly reviews.

questions.explanation: The article treats subscriptions, resource groups, and tags as the accountability coordinates that let cost reports separate owners and environments. The closest distractor is the DNS standard because humans do infer meaning from names, but names alone are weaker than stable tag and scope data when reports must be grouped and filtered. Secret versions and NSG priorities are unrelated to cost allocation. Official docs: [Common uses for Cost Analysis](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/cost-management-billing/costs/cost-analysis-common-uses) and [Use tags to organize your Azure resources and management hierarchy](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-resource-manager/management/tag-resources).

questions.scenario: A cost anomaly points to `vm-prod-02`, but the VM has no `owner` or `service` tag and sits in a mixed-use resource group. Three teams each say the machine might support their workload. What should the cleanup plan improve first so the next review has credible ownership evidence?

options.text: A stricter naming standard for VMs, because cost reviews can infer ownership from resource names even when grouping and tags stay mixed.
A dedicated dashboard filtered to VM resources, because finance can work from a cleaner view even if the source metadata is still incomplete.
A lower anomaly threshold for compute spend, because more alerts reduce the need for explicit ownership evidence.
Required ownership tags plus a resource-group strategy that makes production workload boundaries clear.

questions.explanation: The fix is better ownership metadata and cleaner workload boundaries, not another guess based on names. The closest distractor is the longer VM name because naming helps humans, but the article's point is that tags and grouping make reports reliably filterable and reviewable. Public IPs and budget thresholds do not establish accountability. Official docs: [Use tags to organize your Azure resources and management hierarchy](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-resource-manager/management/tag-resources).

questions.scenario: Azure Advisor recommends downsizing a production SQL database because average CPU looks low. The service owner replies that month-end close drives short but intense spikes, and failed batch windows would breach a finance commitment. What should the reviewer do next?

options.text: Check workload pattern, peak behavior, and business risk before approving the recommendation.
Approve the downsizing if the average CPU remains low, then watch the next month-end close to see whether the workload still fits.
Trial the smaller database in a lower environment first and carry that result into production without checking production peak behavior.
Delay the decision until finance sets a stricter savings target, because cost guidance matters more than the batch workload pattern.

questions.explanation: Advisor recommendations are useful input, but the article makes safe tuning contingent on runtime context and workload promises. The closest distractor is the average-utilization argument, because averages are tempting, yet they can hide the exact peaks that matter for month-end close. Changing the batch window or moving secrets does not answer whether the database can safely run smaller. Official docs: [Optimize cost recommendations in Azure Advisor](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/advisor/advisor-cost-recommendations).

questions.scenario: A month-end review identifies two expensive candidates: a VM scale set that now runs larger instances and a Log Analytics workspace whose retention was increased from 30 to 180 days after a fraud incident. Finance asks for the fastest possible savings, but the report does not include latency targets or investigation requirements. Which two proposed changes require owner validation before approval?

options.text: Reducing VM size or instance count.
Adding a budget alert for the tagged production scope.
Shortening log retention or filtering ingestion.
Backfilling missing owner and environment tags on the affected resources.

questions.explanation: Both of those changes can reduce spend, and both can also break a real workload promise if they ignore operational context. The closest distractor is the budget alert because budgets are valuable for visibility, but they do not change runtime capacity or evidence retention. Dashboard label cleanup is useful housekeeping, not a material tuning decision. Official docs: [Common uses for Cost Analysis](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/cost-management-billing/costs/cost-analysis-common-uses) and [Log Analytics workspace overview](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-monitor/logs/log-analytics-workspace-overview).
